title: SEGMENT 9 — DMR calling and the analytics of methylation

lines.text: SEGMENT 9 — DMR calling and the analytics of methylation
Now let's talk about what you actually do with all this methylation data.
The headline analysis, in almost every methylation study, is differentially methylated region calling — DMR calling.
The question is: given two or more groups of samples, which regions of the genome are differentially methylated between groups?
Tumor versus normal.
Young versus old.
Treated versus untreated.
Healthy versus diseased.
Cell type A versus cell type B.

The answer comes out as a list of genomic regions with associated statistics: this CpG island is hypermethylated in cancer with p-value X, this enhancer is hypomethylated in old age with effect size Y.
The analytics of DMR calling are not trivial, and they have their own subfield of bioinformatics.
Let me walk through the logic, because it matters for how you read any methylation paper.
At the level of a single CpG site, the question is straightforward in principle.
You have, say, ten samples per group, and at each CpG you have a methylation level for each sample — a number between 0 and 1.
You can test whether the methylation levels differ between groups using a standard statistical test — a t-test, a Mann-Whitney, a beta regression.
You get a p-value per CpG.
You correct for multiple testing across all the CpGs you tested — Bonferroni or Benjamini-Hochberg — and you call the CpGs that survive correction as differentially methylated.
This is the single-CpG version of the analysis, often called differentially methylated position or DMP analysis.
It's what you do with EPIC array data when you're looking for individual CpG-level associations.
But methylation often varies in a coordinated way across nearby CpGs.
A CpG island that gets methylated in cancer doesn't just have one CpG go up; it has dozens of CpGs in the same region go up together.
So rather than testing each CpG independently, you can test whether regions — contiguous stretches of CpGs — are differentially methylated as a whole.
This is DMR analysis proper, and it has higher statistical power than per-CpG analysis when the methylation changes are spatially coordinated, which they usually are.
The standard pipeline for DMR calling, in WGBS or RRBS data, looks like this.
You partition the genome into windows, or you scan with a sliding window, identifying contiguous stretches of CpGs that show coordinated methylation differences between groups.

You apply a statistical model — there are many, including BSmooth, methylKit, DSS, dmrseq, and bsseq — that accounts for the binomial nature of methylation count data (each CpG gives you a count of methylated reads out of total reads, not a continuous value) and that smooths or borrows information across nearby CpGs to improve power.
You output a list of DMRs, each with a genomic coordinate, an average methylation difference (often called delta-beta), a statistical significance, and some quality metrics.
The interpretation is then biological.
You annotate the DMRs against gene models — does this DMR overlap a promoter, an enhancer, a gene body?
You annotate against regulatory element atlases — does it overlap an ENCODE-defined enhancer, a CpG island, a chromHMM state?
You ask whether the DMRs are enriched in particular gene sets — are the genes near hypermethylated DMRs enriched for tumor suppressors, for developmental regulators, for immune pathway genes?
The biology comes out of the cross-referencing.
There are several recurring analytical pitfalls that you should know about.
First, batch effects.
Methylation data is notoriously sensitive to batch effects — the day the sample was processed, the array chip position, the bisulfite conversion batch, the operator.
If your cases and controls happen to have been processed on different days, you can get strong but completely artifactual methylation differences.
Standard practice is to randomize cases and controls across batches and to include batch as a covariate in the statistical model.
Ignore this and you'll publish nonsense.
Second, cell-type composition.
Methylation patterns are highly cell-type-specific.

If your samples are mixtures of cell types — for example, whole blood, which contains lymphocytes, monocytes, granulocytes, and others in varying proportions — apparent methylation differences between groups can actually be driven by differences in cell-type composition rather than by within-cell-type methylation changes.
Standard practice is to estimate cell-type proportions from the methylation data itself, using reference deconvolution methods, and adjust for them in the analysis.
Again, ignore this at your peril.
Third, single CpGs are noisy.
Methylation calls at a single CpG, especially from low-coverage WGBS, can be very noisy.
Averaging over regions reduces the noise dramatically and is one of the reasons DMR-based analyses tend to be more reproducible than single-CpG analyses.
Fourth, methylation changes are often small in magnitude.
A 10 percent shift in methylation level can be biologically real and meaningful, especially in mixed cell populations, but it requires careful statistics to distinguish from noise.
Don't expect methylation to flip from 0 to 1; expect it to shift from 0.4 to 0.5, and design your analysis accordingly.

The bottom line on analytics: methylation data is rich, but it has its own statistical character — bounded between 0 and 1, derived from binomial counts, spatially correlated across nearby CpGs, sensitive to batch and cell composition.
Treat it with the right statistical tools and the biology pops out cleanly.
Treat it like generic continuous data and you'll get fooled.
